medications.indication_rationale: Infiltrative local anaesthesia is the correct anaesthetic for in-person primary closure, but it is a clinician-administered procedural drug. The telehealth AI Doctor must NOT recommend or instruct self-injection — it arranges the in-person procedure where the operating clinician provides anaesthesia.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1% with adrenaline, local infiltration of the wound edges (administered by the in-person clinician)
contraindications_in_this_case: Not for self-administration; adrenaline-containing anaesthetic is avoided in end-arterial territories (not relevant to the forearm here)

medications.indication_rationale: A contaminated (kitchen) wound with the last tetanus-containing vaccine >10 years ago is tetanus-prone and requires a booster. Administered at the in-person visit; tetanus immunoglobulin only if the primary immunisation course is incomplete or uncertain.
medications.dose_route_frequency: single intramuscular booster (standard 0.5 mL adult dTpa presentation), given in person

medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for wound pain once the local anaesthetic wears off; the patient can take standard over-the-counter paracetamol at home. No dose is stated in the source note, so none is prescribed here — the AI should direct the patient to the product label / pharmacist.

Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is NOT indicated for a clean, promptly irrigated and closed laceration — recommending it upfront is an error of commission. Oral antibiotics are reserved for an established wound infection (spreading redness, warmth, purulent discharge, fever), which is covered by the safety-net advice, not prescribed pre-emptively.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the closed wound and dressing completely clean and dry for 48 hours; do not submerge the arm in bath or dishwater.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents environmental bacteria from tracking down the suture lines and causing a deep wound infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch the wound daily for increasing redness, warmth, swelling, yellow discharge, red streaking or fever and act on the safety-net advice if they appear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early recognition of wound infection allows prompt oral antibiotics before it becomes a deep infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest and elevate the arm on a pillow tonight and avoid using the hand for heavy or wet tasks until reviewed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation reduces throbbing and swelling; avoiding strain protects the closure while it heals.

patient_education_points.point: This is a deep cut that needs to be cleaned and stitched by a clinician in person today — it cannot be safely closed at home and should not wait, because stitching works best within a short window after the injury.
Until you are seen, press firmly and continuously on the wound through a clean dressing for a full 15 minutes without lifting to peek, and keep the arm raised above heart level.
Because your last tetanus shot was more than 10 years ago and this happened in the kitchen, you will need a tetanus booster at the visit.
After stitching, the numbness from the anaesthetic wears off in 1-2 hours and the area will ache; simple paracetamol usually helps.
Keep the dressing clean and dry for 48 hours; the non-dissolving stitches are usually removed at 7-10 days.

follow_up_plan.interval: 7-10 days for suture removal and wound check (sooner if any concern)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Wound healing and apposition, signs of infection, and removal of the non-absorbable sutures; confirm tetanus prophylaxis was given.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus or fever; fresh heavy bleeding; or any new numbness or loss of finger/wrist movement.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a full-thickness wound needing same-day IN-PERSON primary closure within the suturing window
Instruct correct first-aid haemostasis: firm continuous direct pressure for 15 minutes and elevation of the arm
Arrange urgent same-day face-to-face care (urgent care clinic, GP with minor-procedure capability, or ED)
Screen for arterial, tendon and nerve injury (bleeding character, finger/wrist movement, sensation)
Check tetanus status and flag the need for a booster given >10 years and a contaminated wound
acceptable_alternatives: Referral to an emergency department (T4) is acceptable if no same-day suturing service is available or if bleeding is harder to control
Directing the patient to a suitably equipped after-hours home-visit or urgent-care service that can suture
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person closure (missing the suturing window)
Failing to give pressure/elevation first-aid for haemostasis
Failing to screen for tendon, nerve or arterial injury
errors_of_commission: Instructing the patient to close the wound at home with glue, tape or steri-strips
Instructing self-administration of local anaesthetic or any injected drug
Recommending routine oral antibiotics pre-emptively for a clean wound
Under-triaging to routine self-care or a non-urgent GP appointment days away
Over-ordering imaging for a clean incised wound
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging same-day in-person wound closure AND correct haemostasis first-aid. Advising home self-closure or self-care-only, or failing to escalate to same-day face-to-face care, is an automatic fail regardless of other content.
